request.question: "Ricardio the Heart Guy" is an episode of an animated television series that was created by who?

title: Business Guy
Clown in the Dumps
Family Guy: Back to the Multiverse
Cartoon Wars Part II
The Simpsons Guy
Ricardio the Heart Guy
Adventure Time (season 1)
Lady & Peebles
Brian & Stewie
The Itchy & Scratchy & Poochie Show
sentences: "Business Guy" is the ninth episode in the eighth season of the American animated television series "Family Guy".
It originally aired on Fox in the United States on December 13, 2009.
The episode centers on Peter as he assumes temporary control of his father-in-law Carter Pewterschmidt's billion-dollar empire after he suffers a heart attack and is incapacitated in a coma.

Peter immediately becomes power hungry, however, and develops several ridiculous items, prompting Lois and Carter, after he recovers from his coma, to try to trick him into relinquishing control of the company.
"Clown in the Dumps" is the season premiere of the twenty-sixth season of the American animated television series "The Simpsons", and the 553rd episode of the series overall.
It first aired in the United States on the Fox network on September 28, 2014, with "The Simpsons Guy", a crossover episode of "Family Guy" with "The Simpsons", airing afterwards.
This episode was dedicated in memory of Louis Castellaneta, the father of "The Simpsons" voice actor Dan Castellaneta.
It was written by Joel H. Cohen and directed by Steven Dean Moore, with Don Hertzfeldt directing a sequence in the opening titles.

Jeff Ross, Sarah Silverman and David Hyde Pierce guest starred as themselves, with Jackie Mason and Kelsey Grammer reprising their respective roles as Rabbi Krustofski and Sideshow Bob, while Maurice LaMarche voiced several minor characters.

Family Guy: Back to the Multiverse is an action-adventure game that was released for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 3 and Xbox 360 on November 20, 2012, in North America, November 21, 2012, in Australia and November 23, 2012, in Europe.
The game is based on the American animated television series "Family Guy", most notably the episode "Road to the Multiverse", and is also a continuation of the episode "The Big Bang Theory".
This game also features the return of Stewie's evil half-brother Bertram, who was killed in the show.
"Back to the Multiverse" is the first "Family Guy" console game since "Family Guy Video Game!

When the game was available for pre-order, people who pre-ordered the game received a special level, based on "", another video game based on the "Aliens" trademark also owned by 20th Century Fox, which was released the next February to similar negative reception.
"Cartoon Wars Part II" is the fourth episode in the tenth season of the American animated television series "South Park".
The 143rd episode of the series overall, it first aired on Comedy Central in the United States on April 12, 2006.

After "Cartoon Wars Part I", it is the second part of a two-episode story-arc, which focuses on Cartman's efforts to get the television series "Family Guy" cancelled, by exploiting fears of retaliation by Muslims to an impending "Family Guy" episode in which the Islamic prophet Muhammad will appear, in violation of some interpretations of Muslim law.
Kyle instead urges the president of the network airing "Family Guy", Fox, to air the episode in an exercise of free speech.
"The Simpsons Guy" is the first episode of the thirteenth season of the American animated television series "Family Guy", and the 232nd overall episode.
"The Simpsons Guy" is a 45-minute-long crossover with "The Simpsons", and was written by Patrick Meighan and directed by Peter Shin.
It originally aired in the United States on September 28, 2014, on Fox, where both "The Simpsons" and "Family Guy" have aired since their respective debuts.
"Ricardio the Heart Guy" is the seventh episode of the first season of the American animated television series "Adventure Time".
The episode was written and storyboarded by Bert Youn and Sean Jimenez, from a story by Merriwether Williams, Tim McKeon, and Adam Muto.
It originally aired on Cartoon Network on April 26, 2010.
The episode guest stars George Takei as the title character, Ricardio.
The first season of "Adventure Time", an American animated television series created by Pendleton Ward, premiered on Cartoon Network on April 5, 2010 and concluded on September 27, 2010.
The season was produced by Cartoon Network Studios and Frederator Studios.
The series is based on a short produced for Frederator's Nicktoons Network animation incubator series "Random!
Cartoons".
The season follows the adventures of Finn, a human boy, and his best friend and adoptive brother Jake, a dog with magical powers to change shape and size at will.

Finn and Jake live in the post-apocalyptic Land of Ooo, where they interact with the other main characters of the show: Princess Bubblegum, The Ice King, Marceline the Vampire Queen, Lumpy Space Princess, and BMO.
"Lady & Peebles" is the nineteenth episode of the fourth season of the American animated television series "Adventure Time".
The episode was written and storyboarded by Cole Sanchez and Rebecca Sugar, from a story by Patrick McHale, Kent Osborne, and Pendleton Ward.
It originally aired on Cartoon Network on August 20, 2012.
The episode guest stars George Takei as Ricardio.
"Brian & Stewie" is the 17th episode of the eighth season of the American animated television series "Family Guy", and 150th episode of the series overall.
It originally aired on Fox in the United States on May 2, 2010.
The episode features anthropomorphic dog Brian and baby Stewie after they are accidentally trapped inside a bank vault over a weekend.
The two are ultimately forced to reveal their true feelings about each other, and eventually go on to question each other's existence and purpose in life.
Brian and Stewie become even closer to each other as time goes on, and climactically help each other survive being trapped inside the vault.

The bottle episode breaks from the show's usual set-up, and is the only episode of the series not to feature any music or use any cutaway gags with Brian and Stewie being the only two characters featured in the entire episode.
In repeats of the episode there is no main title sequence, nor is any music played over the end credits.
"The Itchy & Scratchy & Poochie Show" is the fourteenth episode in the eighth season of the American animated television series "The Simpsons".
It originally aired on the Fox network in the United States on February 9, 1997.
In the episode, "The Itchy & Scratchy Show" attempts to regain viewers by introducing a new character named Poochie, whose voice is provided by Homer.
The episode is largely self-referential and satirizes the world of television production, fans of "The Simpsons", and the series itself.
It was written by David X. Cohen and directed by Steven Dean Moore.

Alex Rocco is a credited guest voice as Roger Meyers, Jr. for the third and final time (having previously provided the character's voice in "Itchy & Scratchy & Marge" and "The Day the Violence Died"); Phil Hartman also guest stars as Troy McClure.
Poochie would become a minor recurring character and Comic Book Guy's catchphrase, "Worst episode ever", is introduced in this episode.
With "The Itchy & Scratchy & Poochie Show", the show's 167th episode, "The Simpsons" surpassed "The Flintstones" in the number of episodes produced for a prime-time animated series.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: "Ricardio the Heart Guy" is an episode of an animated television series that was created by who?
hop1_docs: Ricardio the Heart Guy | "Ricardio the Heart Guy" is the seventh episode of the first season of the American animated television series "Adventure Time".  The episode was written and storyboarded by Bert Youn and Sean Jimenez, from a story by Merriwether Williams, Tim McKeon, and Adam Muto.  It originally aired on Cartoon Network on April 26, 2010.  The episode guest stars George Takei as the title character, Ricardio.

Lady & Peebles | "Lady & Peebles" is the nineteenth episode of the fourth season of the American animated television series "Adventure Time".  The episode was written and storyboarded by Cole Sanchez and Rebecca Sugar, from a story by Patrick McHale, Kent Osborne, and Pendleton Ward.  It originally aired on Cartoon Network on August 20, 2012.  The episode guest stars George Takei as Ricardio.

SantApprentice | SantApprentice is an animated television series in 50 episodes of 12 minutes and two 26 minute episodes created by Jan Van Rijsselberge and made by Alphanim (now Gaumont Animation) in 2006.  The holiday series follows the adventures of Nicolas, a young orphan boy from Sydney, Australia, who is a pure of heart and believes in Santa Claus as he is Santa's Apprentice.  Nicolas's job is to achieve his tasks to become full-fledged Santa.

List of Family Guy writers | The following is a list of writers who have worked on the Fox animated television series "Family Guy" in the order of first credited episode (by broadcast).  As of 7 December 2014, 60 people have been credited with writing or co-writing at least one episode of "Family Guy".

You Can't Do That on Television, Peter | "You Can't Do That on Television, Peter" is the eighteenth episode of the tenth season of the animated comedy series, "Family Guy".  The episode originally aired on Fox in the United States on April 1, 2012.  In the episode, Peter creates and stars in a children's TV show after his favourite show is cancelled.  Meanwhile, Meg shadows Dr. Hartman at the Quahog hospital.

Business Guy | "Business Guy" is the ninth episode in the eighth season of the American animated television series "Family Guy".  It originally aired on Fox in the United States on December 13, 2009.  The episode centers on Peter as he assumes temporary control of his father-in-law Carter Pewterschmidt's billion-dollar empire after he suffers a heart attack and is incapacitated in a coma.  Peter immediately becomes power hungry, however, and develops several ridiculous items, prompting Lois and Carter, after he recovers from his coma, to try to trick him into relinquishing control of the company.

Steve Marmel | Steve Marmel (born December 17, 1964) is an American television writer and producer who has worked on many animated TV series, including "The Fairly OddParents", "I Am Weasel", "Danny Phantom", "Family Guy" and "Yin Yang Yo! ".  During his work on "The Fairly OddParents" he frequently co-wrote episodes with Butch Hartman.  Marmel also created the series "Sonny with a Chance" as well as the series "Mech-X4".
hop1_titles: You Can't Do That on Television, Peter
response.summary_1:
- [1] "Ricardio the Heart Guy" — "the seventh episode" (paraphrase: of the American animated series Adventure Time).  
- [1] "The episode guest stars George Takei as the title character, Ricardio."  
- [1] Episode credits: written/storyboarded by Bert Youn and Sean Jimenez; story by Merriwether Williams, Tim McKeon, and Adam Muto.  
- [2] Another Adventure Time episode credit lists Pendleton Ward among story authors ("from a story by ... Pendleton Ward").  

Bridge entity and missing fact(s):
- Bridge entity: Adventure Time (the animated TV series).  
- Missing/ambiguous: The passages do NOT state who "created" Adventure Time. Pendleton Ward appears as a story author ([2]) but the creator credit is not present and must not be assumed.

Next-hop retrieval targets (prioritized):
1) Fetch the "Adventure Time" main page (TV series) — extract the infobox/lead "Created by" field and the lead sentence (or "Production/Development" section) to obtain the series' creator(s) and any co-creator credits.  
2) Fetch the "Pendleton Ward" page — extract the lead/infobox lines that state his role (e.g., "creator of Adventure Time") to confirm creator attribution.  
3) If the above disagree or list multiple names, fetch the "Adventure Time production" or "Credits" section (or episode/series credits) to resolve co-creator vs. contributor distinctions.

No final answer given: required "created by" fact for Adventure Time is absent from the supplied passages.
hop2_docs: Mia's Math Adventure: Just in Time! | Mia's Math Adventure: Just in Time!  is the third title of the "Mia's Big Adventure Collection" software series created by Kutoka Interactive.  Released in 2001 in Canada and the United States, the game teaches mathematics to children between 6 and 10 years old.

Time Gentlemen, Please! (video game) | Time Gentlemen, Please!  is a point-and-click adventure game developed by Zombie Cow Studios.  It is the sequel to the freeware game "Ben There, Dan That! " and was commercially released on June 22, 2009.  As with the first game, "Time Gentlemen, Please!"  was created using the Adventure Game Studio engine.

Adventure Time (pilot) | "Adventure Time" is an animated short created by Pendleton Ward, as well as the pilot to the Cartoon Network series of the same name.  The short follows the adventures of Pen (voiced by Zack Shada), a human boy, and his best friend Jake (voiced by John DiMaggio), a dog with magical powers to change shape and grow and shrink at will.  In this episode, Pen and Jake have to rescue Princess Bubblegum (voiced by Paige Moss) from the antagonistic Ice King (voiced by John Kassir).

The Labyrinth of Time | The Labyrinth of Time is a graphic adventure computer game created by Terra Nova Development, a two-man team composed of Bradley W. Schenck and Michal Todorovic.  Intended to be the first in a series of games, "The Labyrinth of Time" was less successful than similar graphic adventures released around the same time, such as "The 7th Guest" and "Myst".  It is the sole game produced by Terra Nova Development.  In the years after its release, "The Labyrinth of Time" was published on more recent platforms by The Wyrmkeep Entertainment Co. in collaboration with the original developers.

Future-Worm! | Future-Worm!  is an American comic science fiction animated television series created by Ryan Quincy, who previously created IFC's "Out There", for Disney XD.  The show features the adventures of Danny and Future-Worm, who time travel by using a lunch box-shaped time machine.

Ark of Time | Ark of Time is a 1997 adventure game.  Originally created and distributed across Europe, it was later published by Koei Corporation and developed by Trecision S.p.A. in the United States.

The Sarah Jane Adventures | The Sarah Jane Adventures is a British science fiction television programme, that was produced by BBC Cymru Wales for CBBC, created by Russell T Davies starring Elisabeth Sladen.  The programme is a spin-off of the long-running BBC science fiction programme "Doctor Who" and is aimed at a younger audience than "Doctor Who".  It focuses on the adventures of Sarah Jane Smith, an investigative journalist who, as a young woman, had numerous adventures across time and space.
hop2_titles: Mia's Math Adventure: Just in Time!
response.summary_2: Adventure Time was created by Pendleton Ward. Pendleton Ward created the animated short "Adventure Time," which served as the pilot for the Cartoon Network series.
